questions.question: Explain the working principle of Selection Sort with an example array [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]. Show the array after first two passes.
keyPoints: Selection Sort finds minimum from unsorted portion and swaps with first unsorted position
Pass 1: Minimum 11 found, swapped with 64 → [11, 25, 12, 22, 64]
Pass 2: Minimum 12 found in remaining, swapped with 25 → [11, 12, 25, 22, 64]
Process repeats until sorted

questions.question: Derive the time complexity of Selection Sort. Why is it considered O(n²) even when the array is already sorted?
keyPoints: Outer loop runs n-1 times
Inner loop runs n-1, n-2, n-3... times for each pass
Total comparisons = (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 = O(n²)
Even sorted array requires same comparisons to find minimum

questions.question: Why is Selection Sort considered unstable? Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Unstable means relative order of equal elements may change
When swapping minimum element, it may jump over equal elements
Example: [2a, 1, 2b] → after sorting becomes [1, 2b, 2a]
2a and 2b have swapped positions

questions.question: Compare Selection Sort and Bubble Sort. Under what conditions would you prefer Selection Sort?
keyPoints: Both are O(n²) but differ in swaps: Bubble Sort may swap many times per pass, Selection Sort only once per pass
Selection Sort: minimum n-1 swaps guaranteed
Prefer Selection Sort when write operations are expensive (EEPROM, flash memory)
Bubble Sort better for nearly sorted arrays due to early termination

questions.question: Write an algorithm to implement Selection Sort in descending order.
keyPoints: Modify to find maximum instead of minimum in each pass
Swap maximum with first element of unsorted portion
Reduce unsorted portion size by one each iteration
Same O(n²) complexity as ascending version

questions.question: What is the space complexity of Selection Sort? Explain why it is O(1) despite requiring temporary variables for swapping.
keyPoints: Space complexity measures extra memory relative to input size
Only a few variables needed: min_index, i, j, temp
These are constant regardless of array size n
Hence O(1) auxiliary space

questions.question: Prove or disprove: Selection Sort is an adaptive sorting algorithm.
keyPoints: Adaptive algorithms improve performance on partially sorted data
Selection Sort always performs same number of comparisons regardless of initial arrangement
Already sorted array requires n(n-1)/2 comparisons just like reverse sorted
Therefore Selection Sort is NOT adaptive

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement Selection Sort. Include functions for user input, display, and sorting. Trace through the algorithm with an example of 5 elements showing all passes.
keyPoints: Include necessary headers and main function
Create functions: inputArray(), displayArray(), selectionSort()
Implement swap logic using temporary variable
Show complete trace: initial array, array after each swap
Display sorted array and complexity analysis

questions.question: Analyze Selection Sort completely: time complexity (best, worst, average), space complexity, stability, adaptability, and number of swaps. Support your answer with mathematical derivations.
keyPoints: Time: All cases O(n²) - prove using summation of arithmetic series
Space: O(1) - only constant extra variables
Stability: Not stable - explain with example
Adaptability: Not adaptive - same work for all inputs
Swaps: Maximum n-1, minimum 0
Compare with other O(n²) algorithms

questions.question: Explain how Selection Sort can be optimized for sorting in both ascending and descending order using a single function. Write the modified algorithm and analyze its complexity.
keyPoints: Use comparison function pointer or flag for order
In each pass, select appropriate extreme value
Analyze: complexity remains O(n²)
Discuss practical applications and trade-offs
Show C code implementation

questions.question: Compare Selection Sort with Insertion Sort and Bubble Sort. Under what specific scenarios would you recommend each algorithm? Provide justification with time complexity analysis.
keyPoints: All three are O(n²) but with different characteristics
Selection Sort: minimum writes, not stable, not adaptive
Insertion Sort: stable, adaptive, better for nearly sorted
Bubble Sort: stable, can detect sortedness early
Recommend based on: data size, initial order, write cost, stability needs
